title: The phone box library
summary: An old red phone box in a Welsh village is now the smallest library in the country. People leave books — and sometimes letters.
body:
In a small village in Wales, there is an old red phone box. The phone is not there anymore. The phone left ten years ago. But the box is still there.

Now the box is a library. It is the smallest library in Britain. There are one hundred and twenty books inside. The books sit on three small shelves. The door of the phone box still opens and closes. People go in. People go out.

The rules are easy. You take one book. You bring one book. You do not pay anything. Children's books are on the bottom shelf. Old books are on the top shelf. There are books in English. There are books in Welsh. There are five books in Polish, because one family came from Poland.

Some people leave letters in the books. They write, 'I read this book on a hard day. It helped me. I hope it helps you too.' Other people read the letters and write back.

Mrs Owen takes care of the box. She is sixty-four years old. Once a week she cleans the floor. She fixes the books with tape if a page is loose. She does not put new rules on the wall. 'The box,' she says, 'is older than rules.'

In winter the box is cold. In summer the box is hot. People still come.

annotations.meaningVi: bốt điện thoại (kiểu cũ ngoài đường)
các kệ / giá sách (số nhiều của shelf)
chăm sóc / trông nom (cụm động từ)
mỗi tuần một lần (cụm thời gian cố định)
viết thư trả lời (cụm động từ)
ngày khó khăn / ngày mệt mỏi (cụm từ cảm xúc)

grammarNotes.title: There is / There are
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Khi nói có cái gì ở một nơi nào đó, dùng 'There is' (số ít) hoặc 'There are' (số nhiều). 'There is an old red phone box.' 'There are one hundred and twenty books inside.' 'There are five books in Polish.' Câu phủ định: 'There is not / There are not'. Câu hỏi: 'Is there…? / Are there…?'
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use 'There is' for one thing and 'There are' for more than one. 'There is an old red phone box.' 'There are one hundred and twenty books inside.' Negative: 'There is not / There are not.' Question: 'Is there…? / Are there…?'

translationVi:
Trong một ngôi làng nhỏ ở xứ Wales, có một bốt điện thoại đỏ kiểu cũ. Điện thoại không còn ở đó nữa. Điện thoại đã được mang đi mười năm trước. Nhưng cái bốt vẫn còn đó.

Giờ cái bốt là một thư viện. Đó là thư viện nhỏ nhất nước Anh. Bên trong có một trăm hai mươi cuốn sách. Sách xếp trên ba cái kệ nhỏ. Cánh cửa bốt điện thoại vẫn mở ra đóng vào được. Người vào. Người ra.

Quy định đơn giản. Bạn lấy một cuốn. Bạn để lại một cuốn. Bạn không phải trả gì. Sách thiếu nhi để kệ dưới cùng. Sách cũ để kệ trên cùng. Có sách tiếng Anh. Có sách tiếng Wales. Có năm cuốn tiếng Ba Lan, vì một gia đình từng đến từ Ba Lan.

Một số người để lại thư trong sách. Họ viết: 'Tôi đọc cuốn này vào một ngày khó. Nó giúp tôi. Mong nó cũng giúp được bạn.' Những người khác đọc thư và viết trả lời.

Bà Owen chăm sóc cái bốt. Bà sáu mươi tư tuổi. Mỗi tuần một lần, bà quét sàn. Bà dán băng dính nếu sách bị tuột trang. Bà không dán quy định mới lên tường. 'Cái bốt này,' bà nói, 'già hơn cả quy định.'

Mùa đông trong bốt lạnh. Mùa hè trong bốt nóng. Người ta vẫn đến.

options: In a small village in Wales
questions.explanation: 'In a small village in Wales, there is an old red phone box.'

questions.prompt: Is there still a phone inside the box?
options: No — the phone left ten years ago.
No — it was never a phone box.

questions.prompt: How many books are in the library?
questions.explanation: 'There are one hundred and twenty books inside.'

options: Take one book, bring one book, and you don't pay anything.
Only Welsh books may be taken.
questions.explanation: 'You take one book. You bring one book. You do not pay anything.'

questions.prompt: Why are there books in Polish?
options: A school in Poland sent them.
questions.explanation: 'There are five books in Polish, because one family came from Poland.'

questions.prompt: What do some people leave inside the books?
questions.explanation: 'Some people leave letters in the books.'

questions.prompt: What do the letters often say?
options: I read this on a hard day; it helped me; I hope it helps you too.
Do not write in this book.
questions.explanation: The letter writers thank the book for help and pass it on.
questions.hint: Look at the example letter quoted in the text.

questions.prompt: Who takes care of the phone box library?
questions.explanation: 'Mrs Owen takes care of the box.'

questions.prompt: What does Mrs Owen mean by 'The box is older than rules'?
options: The box is from the last century, so the village trusts it without new written rules.
Rules are illegal in the village.
She wants to take the box away.
questions.explanation: She does not put new rules on the wall — the box has been there a long time and runs on trust.
questions.hint: Look at the line before her quote.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: An old village object can become a small, shared, trust-based place — without much explaining.
questions.explanation: The phone box has changed its purpose and is held together by an unwritten agreement.
questions.hint: Notice the simple, unwritten rules and Mrs Owen's quote.

blanks.explanation: The classic red British phone box.
The phone left ten years ago.
It is now a tiny library.
Books sit on three small shelves.
Five books in Polish — one family came from Poland.
Some people leave small letters in the books.
Once a week she cleans the floor.
People still come — in hot and cold weather.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The phone box library has almost no written rules — just 'take one, bring one,' and an old woman who fixes loose pages. Is there a place in your neighbourhood that works on quiet trust like this? What would change if someone tried to make it more 'official'?
